EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A unseasonably cold upper level system brought snow, or sleet that changed to snow to much of western Iowa on May 1st into the early morning hours of May 2nd. Snow and sleet intensities were enhanced by scattered thunderstorms that moved across the area. Snowfall totals of 1 to 3 inches fell over most of the region with a swath of 3 to 5 inches reported from Pottawattamie county north through Harlan, Little Sioux and Castana with even an isolated report of around 6 inches a little east of Missouri Valley.  |The snow and sleet created slushy and rutted roads and winds gusting over 20 mph combined with poor visibilities in moderate snow making for a period of difficult driving conditions which caused some vehicles to slide into ditches, especially along I29 north of Council Bluffs.

Thunderstorms developed along a stationary front during the late evening of April 29th into the early morning hours of the 30th. Some of these storms produced large hail across portions of Fayette and Clayton Counties. Golf ball sized hail was reported in Strawberry Point (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to golf ball sized hail was estimated to have fallen in Strawberry Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As low pressure aloft deepened from western Nebraska into South Dakota, thunderstorms developed across central and eastern Nebraska during the early to mid afternoon hours.  Temperatures climbed into the 70s and 80s and dew point  temperatures in the 60s created moderately unstable conditions across the region.  Several lines of severe thunderstorms moved across the area producing some wind damage along with several reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter-size hail was reported from the same super cell thunderstorm that caused wind damage in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts at several locations in northwest Iowa on the morning of June 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including several trees blown down. The winds also caused roof damage to homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow became heavy in part of northwest Iowa during the afternoon of March 10th, and continued into the nighttime hours. Accumulations as high as 14 inches were reported. Northerly winds were gusting to 30 mph at the start of the snowfall, but decreased slowly during the event and only modest drifting was reported. Classes at some schools were delayed or cancelled on March 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 6 to 14 inches, including 14 inches at Cherokee. Classes at schools were cancelled or delayed on March 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture streamed north into the state through the day on the 20th.  The overall dynamics were weak.  A line of thunderstorms developed in the deeper moisture and produced heavy rain and some hail.  Quarter size hail was reported from one of the stronger storms over Wayne County.

The storm track shifted a bit further north in June, keeping the excessive rains confined to about the northeast one-third of the state. The month���s largest event|came from the 21st to the 23rd with Dorchester recording 8.99 inches of rain. Major|flooding resulted along northeast Iowa rivers such as the Upper Iowa and the Turkey. A second major rain event on the 24th brought 6.80 inches of rain to Lowden and resulted in major flooding along the Wapsipinicon River. Rain totals for the month varied from 2.11 inches at Holly Springs (Woodbury County) and 2.22 inches near Albia to a whopping 14.06 inch total at Dorchester (but still shy of their June record total of 15.13 inches set in 2008). The persistent spring and early summer precipitation has allowed all of Iowa to be rated as drought-free by the U. S. Drought Monitor for the first time since early August 2011. According to Iowa Agricultural Statistics crop planting progress has been the slowest since 1993 with 4% of the soybean acreage, mostly in north central and northeast Iowa, still unplanted at month���s end. The statewide average year-to-date precipitation total was a record for the first one-half of the year at 25.07 inches or 7.87 inches more than normal. The old record for January through June was 24.46 inches set in 2008 while 2012���s total for the entire year was 26.49 inches.  Flooding in the Des Moines CWA was limited to the northeast, or Cedar River system.  Flood waters rose 2 to 5 feet above flood stage in those areas.

Isolated weak showers developed during the afternoon and evening of June 7th over northeast Iowa along a nearly stationary trough of low pressure. Conditions were favorable for funnel clouds to develop despite the showers being very weak. No touchdowns were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was spotted southeast of Waucoma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated weak showers developed during the afternoon and evening of June 7th over northeast Iowa along a nearly stationary trough of low pressure. Conditions were favorable for funnel clouds to develop despite the showers being very weak. No touchdowns were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Funnel clouds were sighted near New Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple rounds of severe thunderstorms rolled across northeast Iowa during the afternoon and evening of June 12th. These storms developed ahead of an area of low pressure that was moving east across the Upper Midwest. The storms primarily produced high winds that blew down trees near Marquette (Clayton County) in the afternoon and around Nora Springs and Marble Rock (Floyd County) in the evening. Some quarter sized hail also fell in Oran (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down northwest of Marble Rock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 4 to 10 inches covered portions of northeast Iowa from a winter storm that moved through the region on January 30th. The storm originated over the southern Plains states and moved northeast through the Mississippi River Valley producing a blanket of heavy snow from central and eastern Iowa into Wisconsin. In addition to the snow, the storm produced northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph that created blowing and drifting snow that at times, lowered the visibility to a quarter mile. The timing of the storm, had a direct impact on the morning commute of the 30th. The highest reported snowfall amount was 10 inches near Oelwein (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and blowing snow created hazardous conditions across the county, especially during the morning commute time. The visibility at times dropped as low a quarter of a mile in the snow and blowing snow. The highest reported snowfall total was 5.5 inches in Dorchester.

These storms moved east across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa and produced isolated areas of wind damage.

Thunderstorms developed near a stationary front in northern Nebraska early in the afternoon of August 1. The storms intensified and moved southeast through the evening and the strongest storms remained in Nebraska.  However, in response to the thunderstorms to the east, strong gradient winds spread over southwest Iowa causing at least one strong wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gradient winds spread over parts of southwest Iowa in response to thunderstorms over southeast Nebraska.  A wind gust of 67 mph was measured by the Awos in Red Oak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused flooding of the Little Sioux and Ocheyedan Rivers in northwest Iowa from May 26th to May 30th. The flooding was major to record along parts of the Little Sioux River with the most significant impact in the Cherokee area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor to moderate flooding of the Ocheyedan River near Spencer. Lowlands including a few roads were affected. The river crested at 2.0 feet above flood stage near Spencer on May 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy precipitation with rapid cooling produced a combination of freezing rain, sleet, and snow over much of northwest Iowa from the evening hours of April 9th, to the morning of April 11th. Moderate to heavy ice accumulations were reported mostly near the northern border of the state. This was followed by light to moderate snow and sleet accumulations. The ice accumulations, greater than a half inch in some areas, caused major tree and power line damage in the ice storm area. Fallen branches and limbs were numerous, trees were destroyed, roads were blocked, and some vehicles and homes suffered damages from the falling trees, limbs, and branches. There were also major power outages because of the power line and power pole damage. Strong winds with and following the storm, not strong enough to cause significant damage under dry conditions, contributed greatly to the tree and power line damage. The snow and sleet accumulations made travel difficult, especially in areas where downed trees and power lines were already causing road blockages. Both ice and snow accumulations were less, and caused fewer problems, further south, including the Sioux City to Storm Lake areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of precipitation began with freezing rain quickly producing heavy ice accumulations, ranging up to between a quarter and half inch. The precipitation then changed to sleet and then snow, with sleet and snow accumulations reaching 6 inches near Sibley. Many branches, limbs, whole trees, and power lines were downed by the weight of ice and accompanying wind. Both urban areas and rural electric cooperatives suffered major power line damage. Trees and tree debris blocked many roads, and damaged several vehicles and homes. Major power outages were reported, affecting thousands of people. The winter precipitation made travel impossible in many areas, resulting in schools and businesses being forced to close.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused the big Sioux River to flood along most of the Big Sioux River in northwest Iowa in late May. The flooding briefly reached major status in the Akron area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding along the Big Sioux River, with lowlands including some farmland affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused the big Sioux River to flood along most of the Big Sioux River in northwest Iowa in late May. The flooding briefly reached major status in the Akron area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor to briefly major flooding of the Big Sioux River affected a significant amount of farmland, with other lowlands and several roads also being flooded. The river crested at 4.47 feet above flood stage at Akron on May 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused major to record flooding of the Floyd River from Alton to James in late May. Farmland, other lowlands, roads, and basements and lower levels of homes and businesses were flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused major flooding on the Floyd River. Significant farmland was flooded and some crops were destroyed or deemed to need replanting. Other lowlands along the river were flooded, and several roads were flooded. The basements or lower levels of several homes and business were flooded. The river crested at 5.2 feet above flood stage near Merrill on May 28th.

There were also major power outages because of the power line and power pole damage. Strong winds with and following the storm, not strong enough to cause significant damage under dry conditions, contributed greatly to the tree and power line damage. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of light rain and drizzle changed to freezing rain and freezing drizzle, producing light icing. The precipitation then changed to snow and sleet, with an accumulation of 3.5 inches at Sioux City. The winter made travel hazardous and closed or delayed some schools.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain which caused flash flooding in Sioux, O'Brien, and Cherokee Counties in northwest Iowa on the morning of May 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of up to 5 inches in a short time caused significant flooding of streets, and flooding of basements or lower levels of a few homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wet snow, mixed with rain at times, accumulated 3 to 6 inches in several counties in northwest Iowa on May 1st into the early morning hours of May 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wet snow, mixed at times with rain, accumulated 3 to 6 inches, including 5 inches at Lester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wet snow, mixed with rain at times, accumulated 3 to 6 inches in several counties in northwest Iowa on May 1st into the early morning hours of May 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wet snow, mixed at times with rain, accumulated 3 to 6 inches, including 6 inches 3 miles east of Sibley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along and north of slow moving cold front during the evening of August 11.  Due to the moisture and instability present, a few of the storms produced marginally severe size hail in eastern Nebraska along brief periods of heavy rain which caused isolated flash flooding in a few communities near the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused some minor flash flooding of a few streets in the Onawa area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy precipitation with rapid cooling produced a combination of freezing rain, sleet, and snow over much of northwest Iowa from the evening hours of April 9th, to the morning of April 11th. Moderate to heavy ice accumulations were reported mostly near the northern border of the state. This was followed by light to moderate snow and sleet accumulations. The ice accumulations, greater than a half inch in some areas, caused major tree and power line damage in the ice storm area. Fallen branches and limbs were numerous, trees were destroyed, roads were blocked, and some vehicles and homes suffered damages from the falling trees, limbs, and branches. There were also major power outages because of the power line and power pole damage. Strong winds with and following the storm, not strong enough to cause significant damage under dry conditions, contributed greatly to the tree and power line damage. The snow and sleet accumulations made travel difficult, especially in areas where downed trees and power lines were already causing road blockages. Both ice and snow accumulations were less, and caused fewer problems, further south, including the Sioux City to Storm Lake areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of precipitation began with freezing rain quickly producing heavy ice accumulations, ranging up to between a quarter and half inch. The precipitation then changed to sleet and then snow, with sleet and snow accumulations reaching 3 to 6 inches. Many branches, limbs, whole trees, and power lines were downed by the weight of ice and accompanying wind. Both urban areas and rural electric cooperatives suffered major power line damage. Trees and tree debris blocked many roads, and damaged several vehicles and homes. Major power outages were reported, affecting thousands of people. The winter precipitation made travel impossible in many areas, resulting in schools and businesses being forced to close.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple round of thunderstorms moved across northeast Iowa during the afternoon and evening of May 31st. These storms developed in a complex surface pattern across the Upper Midwest as a pre-frontal surface trough, dry line and cold front all passed through the area. A small tornado touched down north of Floyd (Floyd County) and quarter sized hail fell in St. Ansgar (Mitchell County), otherwise, the storms passed through without producing much severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Funnel clouds were sighted along the Mitchell County line between Riceville and Elma.

An upper level disturbance tracking across the plains caused low pressure to deepen as it tracked from northern Oklahoma into northeast Missouri.  Snow, occasionally moderate and even briefly heavy, fell from late on the 29th into the morning hours of the 30th.  Snow amounts in southwest Iowa were generally 3 to 6 inches, with around 6 inches reported from Glenwood through Shenandoah, Iowa.  The snow was accompanied by north winds of 20 to 35 which caused considerable blowing and drifting snow and several hours of poor visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Glenwood reported 6 inches of snow which probably represented amounts over much of Mills county.

A strong area of low pressure moved out of the Southern Plains into the Ohio River Valley from December 21st through the 22nd. Heavy snow on the northwest side of this system fell over a small part of northeast Iowa with a band of 6 inches falling across the southern sections of Clayton County. The highest reported totals from Clayton County were 6 inches in Edgewood and Guttenberg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported between 4 and 6 inches of snow across Clayton County. The highest totals occurred across the southern part of the county where 6 inches was measured in both Edgewood and Guttenberg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved into the central Plains on Saturday morning December 21st. This storm system brought widespread significant ice accumulation to parts of the southern Plains to the upper Mississippi River Valley. To the north of the icing, a swatch of heavy snow extended from central Kansas northeast to eastern Iowa, include parts of southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa. Snow spread into the area late on Saturday afternoon, becoming heavy during the evening, and tapered to flurries by Sunday morning as the storm system moved off to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 3 to 6 inches fell across all of Fremont County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued over all of northwest Iowa in February, despite precipitation which was a little above normal. The excess of a few tenths of an inch in the driest month of the year did little to relieve the long term dry soil conditions. There was little noted in the way of new effects of the drought, with the dry conditions giving a poor outlook for the Spring and Summer, including the poor germination of the winter wheat crop during the dry fall. Water restrictions continued to be few during the winter because of the low water usage, but the area was deemed vulnerable to even marginally dry weather if it developed in the spring and summer. Drought was generally listed as continued extreme to exceptional for the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions continued, despite above normal precipitation, due to the excess being only a few tenths of an inch during the normally driest month. Few new effects were noted, with the seasonally low water usage. The main problem, aside from winter livestock feed, was the poor outlook for the spring and summer, which were expected to be extremely sensitive to dry weather.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued over all of northwest Iowa in March. Though precipitation was near normal, due mainly to one snowstorm, drought was still rated severe to exceptional over all of the area. There continued to be a lack of soil moisture as winter approached its end, since even normal winter precipitation is low compared to the warmer seasons. Also, the frozen ground forced runoff of what precipitation and snowmelt there was. There was little noted in the way of new effects of the drought, with the dry conditions giving a poor outlook for the Spring and Summer, including the winter wheat crop, which was also affected by poor germination conditions in the dry fall. Water restrictions continued to be few because of the low water usage, but the area was deemed vulnerable to even marginally dry weather if it developed in the spring and summer. Some effect was noted on livestock, although the majority of the livestock was in good condition despite persistent feed shortages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions continued in March, with runoff over frozen ground leaving soil moisture conditions very dry, despite near normal precipitation. Few new effects were noted, though the dry conditions coming out of winter were expected to immediately affect the winter wheat crop, as they had affected germination in the fall. Seasonally low water usage resulted in few other new effects. The main problem, aside from winter livestock feed, was the poor outlook for the spring and summer, which were expected to be extremely sensitive to dry weather.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought conditions ended across northeast Iowa over the first half of March. Despite these improvements, all or parts of Floyd, Howard and Mitchell Counties were still in a severe drought. Snowfall amounts average 14 to 18 inches with 2.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation in the counties affected by the drought. Enough of this moisture was absorbed by the soil for the improvements to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across Howard County averaged 16 to 18 inches with 2.5 to 2.75 inches of liquid precipitation during March. With the frozen ground, little to none of this moisture was absorbed by the soil. No change in the drought conditions occurred with the northwest corner of the county remaining in a severe drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable airmass on May 19, 2013 led to the development of severe thunderstorms that produced large hail and damaging winds. Temperatures were in the 80s with dewpoints around 70 with a mid level speed maximum rotating around the base of a developing upper low in the Plains states. This increased the deep layer shear during the afternoon and evening hours.  Some of the storms produced golf to baseball size hail and 70 to 80 mph winds.  A trained spotter reported baseball size hail knocked out sky lights at the Walmart store in northeast Keokuk. Golfball to baseball size hail was reported on the north end of the city, with nickel to quarter size hail on the south end of the city.  A downburst produced wind damage in Benton county from the county line 3.5 miles southeast of Blairstown to 2.8 miles southeast of Shellsburg. There was a mile wide swath of near continuous damage to farm outbuildings, tree branches and some power poles. The greatest damage was from 3 miles south of Newhall, where a machine shed and garage were completely destroyed, to about 3 miles northwest of Atkins, where significant damage occurred to outbuildings.  The emergency manager in Buchanan county reported a downburst with winds more than 70 mph struck Independence, Iowa. The northwest side of town sustained the most damage with trees down, power poles snapped and signs blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated winds of 65 to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front extended from a surface low pressure in northwest Kansas to just north of Omaha and then into central Illinois on May 29, 2013. Plentiful moisture was noted south of this front with dewpoints in the 60s.  A strengthening low level jet to 50 knots impinging on the front developed numerous thunderstorms across the area. These storms produced damaging wind, large hail and flash flooding.  The emergency manager in Linn county reported the town of Walker sustained major damage from severe flash flooding, mostly due to Blue Creek in town. Between 50 and 100 homes were damaged, a couple of businesses were destroyed and bridges were under water. About 5 inches of rain fell in about 2 hours.

A cold front pushing into a very moist and unstable airmass triggered numerous severe thunderstorms on May 30, 2013.  Deep layer shear was moderate to strong and freezing levels were in the 11-12k foot level, which was ideal for large hail. The storms produced damaging winds, large hail and several weak tornadoes.  An EF1 tornado touched down in Andalusia, IL around 445 pm CDT and traveled due north. This weak tornado crossed the Mississippi River and moved through eastern sections of Buffalo, IA. Wind speeds were estimated to be 95 mph. Some large trees were snapped and uprooted along the path. Some of the fallen trees fell onto houses.  In extreme northeast Missouri, wind gusts estimated to be 70 mph produced heavy tree damage throughout Scotland County. Several outbuildings were damaged and about 30 power poles were snapped. The tree damage was mainly located between Bible Grove and Memphis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving into the Great Lakes allowed for a cold front to move southeast into western Iowa and eastern Nebraska. The atmosphere along and ahead of the cold front become strongly unstable during the afternoon and evening. Scattered thunderstorms developed along and ahead of the cold front and produced isolated severe weather. The thunderstorms quickly diminished during the late evening as the instability decreased.

The greatest damage was from 3 miles south of Newhall, where a machine shed and garage were completely destroyed, to about 3 miles northwest of Atkins, where significant damage occurred to outbuildings.  The emergency manager in Buchanan county reported a downburst with winds more than 70 mph struck Independence, Iowa. The northwest side of town sustained the most damage with trees down, power poles snapped and signs blown down.

A storm system moving into the Great Lakes allowed for a cold front to move southeast into western Iowa and eastern Nebraska. The atmosphere along and ahead of the cold front become strongly unstable during the afternoon and evening. Scattered thunderstorms developed along and ahead of the cold front and produced isolated severe weather. The thunderstorms quickly diminished during the late evening as the instability decreased.

A very unstable airmass on May 19, 2013 led to the development of severe thunderstorms that produced large hail and damaging winds. Temperatures were in the 80s with dewpoints around 70 with a mid level speed maximum rotating around the base of a developing upper low in the Plains states. This increased the deep layer shear during the afternoon and evening hours.  Some of the storms produced golf to baseball size hail and 70 to 80 mph winds.  A trained spotter reported baseball size hail knocked out sky lights at the Walmart store in northeast Keokuk. Golfball to baseball size hail was reported on the north end of the city, with nickel to quarter size hail on the south end of the city. A small tornado developed east of Garnavillo (Clayton County) and traveled east/northeast to the Mississippi River before crossing over into Wisconsin. This tornado was part of a larger severe weather outbreak that occurred across much of northeast Iowa during the afternoon of May 29th. These storms developed in a warm and moist airmass behind a warm front that was moving north across the Upper Midwest. In addition to the tornado, a farm east of Oelwein (Fayette County) was hit hard by straight line winds and flash flooding occurred in Protivin (Howard County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused several small streams that feed the Maquoketa River to go out of their banks south of Arlington. At least three roads were closed due to washouts or water over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into Iowa during the afternoon from the northwest.  The airmass became very unstable ahead of the front.  Temperatures rose into the upper 80s to low 90s, with dewpoint readings in the upper 60s to lower 70s.  CAPE increased to 4000 J/kg by late afternoon with a lifted index between -10 and -18 C.  Downdraft CAPE was between 1200 and 1700 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere in the 700 to 900 J/kg range.  The freezing level was very high, around 15,000 feet, with the LCL at 1250 meters.  Deep moisture was available for the system, with precipitable water values around 1.6 inches.  Little shear was available with only 20 kts of effective shear.  A line of thunderstorms formed along the cold front during the afternoon and tracked southeast into Iowa.  Several of the storms produced hail from the size of quarters to the size of golf balls.  Funnel clouds were also reported with a few of the storms.  As the thunderstorms continued into the later afternoon and evening, wind became a more dominant factor.  Numerous trees were downed by high winds in Wright and Franklin Counties.  The storms developed southwest with a line of significant thunderstorms extending from northeast into central Iowa by early to mid evening as new storms formed on the outflow boundary of the original development.  The storms continued to produce quarter to golf ball size hail and wind gusts of 60 to 70 MPH.  The winds caused spotty building damage, including a barn near Story City.  Six horses that were inside were killed.  The barn was east of Boone, where buildings and power lines were destroyed at the Central Iowa Expo grounds.  Two inch diameter hail was reported in parts of Wright and Boone Counties.  A heat burst produced 73 MPH winds in Marion County at the Knoxville Airport.  The temperature rose from 70 to 84 in 15 minutes, and the dewpoint dropped from 63 to 48 in 10 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree down in a yard several feet in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass was in place over Iowa with CAPE over 2000 J/kg already by early in the morning.  Downdraft CAPE was quite high, in the 1000 to 1300 J/kg range, with 400 to 600 J/kg in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere.  The shear was quite weak, in the 25 to 35 kt range.  Deep moisture was in place with precipitable water values of 1.8 inches.  The LCL was quite low as well, in the 500 to 750 meter range.  There was little hail as the freezing level was quite high at 14,400 feet.  Strong thunderstorms developed in a north to south line across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa.  During the morning the line took on a bow echo appearance as bow beta elements began to push rapidly east.  A swath of 60 to 70 MPH winds took place from east of the Omaha-Council Bulffs area in southwest Iowa all the way east into central Iowa in the Marshalltown and Oskaloosa areas.  The high winds overturned semi-tractortrailer trucks in Guthrie and Greene Counties.  Shingles were also blown from a house in Guthrie Count west of Guthrie Center.  In Audubon County, high winds lifted two farm buildings off of their post foundations and carried them one quarter of a mile away.  A funnel cloud was observed west of Eldora in Hardin County.  Very heavy rainfall occurred over parts of northeast Iowa.  Franklin, Butler, and Grundy Counties were hard hit.  Numerous roads and highways were under water and closed as 4 to over 7 inches of rain fell in under six hours.  The heaviest rainfall total was in the Aplington Area in Butler County.  A total of 7.5 inches fell there.  Severe flooding took place in the New Hartford area.  Near record high river stages prompted the evacuation of the town.  In Parkersburg, 1500 to 2000 feet of railroad tracks were washed out.  There was some hail with the storms.  Most of them had small hail, though no significant hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 3 covered by running water in various places between Hansell and Dumont in Butler County.  Streams out of their banks and fields full of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front extended from a surface low pressure in northwest Kansas to just north of Omaha and then into central Illinois on May 29, 2013. Plentiful moisture was noted south of this front with dewpoints in the 60s.  A strengthening low level jet to 50 knots impinging on the front developed numerous thunderstorms across the area. These storms produced damaging wind, large hail and flash flooding.  The emergency manager in Linn county reported the town of Walker sustained major damage from severe flash flooding, mostly due to Blue Creek in town. Between 50 and 100 homes were damaged, a couple of businesses were destroyed and bridges were under water. About 5 inches of rain fell in about 2 hours.

A cold front pushing into a very moist and unstable airmass triggered numerous severe thunderstorms on May 30, 2013.  Deep layer shear was moderate to strong and freezing levels were in the 11-12k foot level, which was ideal for large hail. The storms produced damaging winds, large hail and several weak tornadoes.  An EF1 tornado touched down in Andalusia, IL around 445 pm CDT and traveled due north. This weak tornado crossed the Mississippi River and moved through eastern sections of Buffalo, IA. Wind speeds were estimated to be 95 mph. Some large trees were snapped and uprooted along the path. Some of the fallen trees fell onto houses.  In extreme northeast Missouri, wind gusts estimated to be 70 mph produced heavy tree damage throughout Scotland County. Several outbuildings were damaged and about 30 power poles were snapped. The tree damage was mainly located between Bible Grove and Memphis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 4 inch diameter trees down in Farmington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure was located in western South Dakota with a stationary front extending into eastern Missouri on May 26-27, 2013.  North of the front temperatures were only in the 50s in eastern Iowa but to the south readings were in the 80s with dewpoints in the 60s. A strengthening low level jet impinging on the frontal boundary helped to trigger numerous training thunderstorms across the area. This led to flash flooding in many areas with roads closed.  In Louisa county a trained spotter reported Highway 92 between Cotter and Columbus Junction had water flowing across it in many locations. One section had large debris washed onto the highway that included railroad ties and logs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported detours from River Drive to Grant Street is flooded in Davenport with vehicles getting stuck in the water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area tracked east out of South Dakota during the afternoon and evening.  A warm front extending from the low across west central into southeast Iowa lifted north with thunderstorm development during the afternoon.  Thunderstorms formed during the late afternoon and overnight hours as the cold front trailing form the low moved into Iowa  The atmosphere became moderately unstable during the afternoon and evening as moisture continued to pool near the warm front and ahead of the low.  Precipitable water values increased to 1.8 inches by evening.  The CAPE increased to 2000 to 3000 J/kg with 600 to 900 J/kg available in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere, though the freezing level was quite high at 15,000 feet.  Downdraft CAPE was in the 800 to 1000 J/kg range.  The atmosphere had 40 to 50 kts of shear available. Thunderstorms moved into western Iowa and became severe.  Initially the storms produced hail.  Most of the hail was in the 1 to 1.5 inch range, with a few locations approaching 2 inch diameter hail.  Upscale development took place with the storms developing into an MCC before midnight.  The storms then became producers of high high winds and heavy rainfall.  A 67 MPH wind gust was recorded in the Carroll area.  One storm west of Pocahontas produced a 2 mile long narrow path of damage as microburst winds estimated around 90 MPH damaged several houses.  Roofing was lost with debris embedded in exterior walls.  Farm equipment was also blown over.  Flash flooding took place in west central Iowa and parts of central Iowa as reports of 3 to 7 inches of rain in some locations produced flash flooding.  Many highways were closed by high water, and countless county roads were flooded in Carroll, Guthrie, Greene, and Madison Counties.  Subsequent flooding in Madison County caused considerable damage with several bridges washed out or underwater northwest of Bevington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage to machinery buildings with one blown over and two others damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable and very moist atmosphere was in place across Iowa with numerous boundaries across the region.  Precipitable water was around 1.8 inches.  About 3000 J/kg of CAPE was available, but severe parameters were relatively weak.  With the deep warm cloud depth in place, thunderstorms were efficient rain producers.  Thunderstorms formed along an upper level boundary in northeast Iowa.  They were in a northwest to southeast orientation, with the thunderstorms training southeast along the line.  Two to four inches of rain fell in parts of Bremer, Butler, and Black Hawk Counties.  The ground was already nearly saturated.  Flash flooding occurred across the area.  There was little in the way of severe weather.  A hail report of quarter size hail was received in Black Hawk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported over Donald Road near Dewar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into northwest Iowa during the mid to late afternoon hours.  The airmass became unstable by the early afternoon with CAPE in the 2000 to 3000 J/kg range.  Downdraft CAPE was between 800 and 1200 J/kg.  The freezing level was around 13,000 feet.  Precipitable water was around 1.3 to 1.4 inches.  Shear was moderate with 30 to 40 kts of effective shear.  The LCL was quite high around 1750 meters due to dry air in the low levels of the atmosphere.  Thunderstorms developed along the cold front over north central Iowa initially.  The storms produced very heavy rainfall with several reports of penny to nickel size hail received.  One storm produced quarter to half dollar size hail in Hancock County.  Storms became more numerous by early evening.  Many produced small hail.  One storm in Pocahontas County produced hail up to quarter size.  Very heavy rainfall occurred over parts of north central Iowa.  The storms produced around 2 inches of rain in an hour.  More than 3 inches of rain fell in the Aplington area of Butler County.

A cold front moved into Iowa during the afternoon from the northwest.  The airmass became very unstable ahead of the front.  Temperatures rose into the upper 80s to low 90s, with dewpoint readings in the upper 60s to lower 70s.  CAPE increased to 4000 J/kg by late afternoon with a lifted index between -10 and -18 C.  Downdraft CAPE was between 1200 and 1700 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere in the 700 to 900 J/kg range.  The freezing level was very high, around 15,000 feet, with the LCL at 1250 meters.  Deep moisture was available for the system, with precipitable water values around 1.6 inches.  Little shear was available with only 20 kts of effective shear.  A line of thunderstorms formed along the cold front during the afternoon and tracked southeast into Iowa.  Several of the storms produced hail from the size of quarters to the size of golf balls.  Funnel clouds were also reported with a few of the storms.  As the thunderstorms continued into the later afternoon and evening, wind became a more dominant factor.  Numerous trees were downed by high winds in Wright and Franklin Counties.  The storms developed southwest with a line of significant thunderstorms extending from northeast into central Iowa by early to mid evening as new storms formed on the outflow boundary of the original development.  The storms continued to produce quarter to golf ball size hail and wind gusts of 60 to 70 MPH.  The winds caused spotty building damage, including a barn near Story City.  Six horses that were inside were killed.  The barn was east of Boone, where buildings and power lines were destroyed at the Central Iowa Expo grounds.  Two inch diameter hail was reported in parts of Wright and Boone Counties.  A heat burst produced 73 MPH winds in Marion County at the Knoxville Airport.  The temperature rose from 70 to 84 in 15 minutes, and the dewpoint dropped from 63 to 48 in 10 minutes.

A low pressure area tracked east out of South Dakota during the afternoon and evening.  A warm front extending from the low across west central into southeast Iowa lifted north with thunderstorm development during the afternoon.  Thunderstorms formed during the late afternoon and overnight hours as the cold front trailing form the low moved into Iowa  The atmosphere became moderately unstable during the afternoon and evening as moisture continued to pool near the warm front and ahead of the low.  Precipitable water values increased to 1.8 inches by evening.  The CAPE increased to 2000 to 3000 J/kg with 600 to 900 J/kg available in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere, though the freezing level was quite high at 15,000 feet.  Downdraft CAPE was in the 800 to 1000 J/kg range.  The atmosphere had 40 to 50 kts of shear available. Thunderstorms moved into western Iowa and became severe.  Initially the storms produced hail.  Most of the hail was in the 1 to 1.5 inch range, with a few locations approaching 2 inch diameter hail.  Upscale development took place with the storms developing into an MCC before midnight.  The storms then became producers of high high winds and heavy rainfall.  A 67 MPH wind gust was recorded in the Carroll area.  One storm west of Pocahontas produced a 2 mile long narrow path of damage as microburst winds estimated around 90 MPH damaged several houses.  Roofing was lost with debris embedded in exterior walls.  Farm equipment was also blown over.  Flash flooding took place in west central Iowa and parts of central Iowa as reports of 3 to 7 inches of rain in some locations produced flash flooding.  Many highways were closed by high water, and countless county roads were flooded in Carroll, Guthrie, Greene, and Madison Counties.  Subsequent flooding in Madison County caused considerable damage with several bridges washed out or underwater northwest of Bevington.

A cold front pushing into a very moist and unstable airmass triggered numerous severe thunderstorms on May 30, 2013.  Deep layer shear was moderate to strong and freezing levels were in the 11-12k foot level, which was ideal for large hail. The storms produced damaging winds, large hail and several weak tornadoes.  An EF1 tornado touched down in Andalusia, IL around 445 pm CDT and traveled due north. This weak tornado crossed the Mississippi River and moved through eastern sections of Buffalo, IA. Wind speeds were estimated to be 95 mph. Some large trees were snapped and uprooted along the path. Some of the fallen trees fell onto houses.  In extreme northeast Missouri, wind gusts estimated to be 70 mph produced heavy tree damage throughout Scotland County. Several outbuildings were damaged and about 30 power poles were snapped. The tree damage was mainly located between Bible Grove and Memphis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb about 10 inches in diameter along with several tree limbs down was reported by a spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable airmass on May 19, 2013 led to the development of severe thunderstorms that produced large hail and damaging winds. Temperatures were in the 80s with dewpoints around 70 with a mid level speed maximum rotating around the base of a developing upper low in the Plains states. This increased the deep layer shear during the afternoon and evening hours.  Some of the storms produced golf to baseball size hail and 70 to 80 mph winds.  A trained spotter reported baseball size hail knocked out sky lights at the Walmart store in northeast Keokuk. Golfball to baseball size hail was reported on the north end of the city, with nickel to quarter size hail on the south end of the city.  A downburst produced wind damage in Benton county from the county line 3.5 miles southeast of Blairstown to 2.8 miles southeast of Shellsburg. There was a mile wide swath of near continuous damage to farm outbuildings, tree branches and some power poles. The greatest damage was from 3 miles south of Newhall, where a machine shed and garage were completely destroyed, to about 3 miles northwest of Atkins, where significant damage occurred to outbuildings.  The emergency manager in Buchanan county reported a downburst with winds more than 70 mph struck Independence, Iowa. The northwest side of town sustained the most damage with trees down, power poles snapped and signs blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported widespread wind damage across much of Johnson county which occurred between 1030 and 1100 pm.  A tree was blown over onto Interstate 80 near mile marker 231.  Winds were estimated at 65 to 70 mph.

A cold front moved into northwest Iowa during the mid to late afternoon hours.  The airmass became unstable by the early afternoon with CAPE in the 2000 to 3000 J/kg range.  Downdraft CAPE was between 800 and 1200 J/kg.  The freezing level was around 13,000 feet.  Precipitable water was around 1.3 to 1.4 inches.  Shear was moderate with 30 to 40 kts of effective shear.  The LCL was quite high around 1750 meters due to dry air in the low levels of the atmosphere.  Thunderstorms developed along the cold front over north central Iowa initially.  The storms produced very heavy rainfall with several reports of penny to nickel size hail received.  One storm produced quarter to half dollar size hail in Hancock County.  Storms became more numerous by early evening.  Many produced small hail.  One storm in Pocahontas County produced hail up to quarter size.  Very heavy rainfall occurred over parts of north central Iowa.  The storms produced around 2 inches of rain in an hour.  More than 3 inches of rain fell in the Aplington area of Butler County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to penny size hail lasted for a total of 5 to 10 minutes.

An area of thunderstorms moved through the area during the previous night.  As the storms moved east an area of high winds developed on the back side of the complex. High tension power lines were blown down southwest of Fort Dodge. An unstable airmass was in place over Iowa with CAPE over 2000 J/kg already by early in the morning.  Downdraft CAPE was quite high, in the 1000 to 1300 J/kg range, with 400 to 600 J/kg in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere.  The shear was quite weak, in the 25 to 35 kt range.  Deep moisture was in place with precipitable water values of 1.8 inches.  The LCL was quite low as well, in the 500 to 750 meter range.  There was little hail as the freezing level was quite high at 14,400 feet.  Strong thunderstorms developed in a north to south line across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa.  During the morning the line took on a bow echo appearance as bow beta elements began to push rapidly east.  A swath of 60 to 70 MPH winds took place from east of the Omaha-Council Bulffs area in southwest Iowa all the way east into central Iowa in the Marshalltown and Oskaloosa areas.  The high winds overturned semi-tractortrailer trucks in Guthrie and Greene Counties.  Shingles were also blown from a house in Guthrie Count west of Guthrie Center.  In Audubon County, high winds lifted two farm buildings off of their post foundations and carried them one quarter of a mile away.  A funnel cloud was observed west of Eldora in Hardin County.  Very heavy rainfall occurred over parts of northeast Iowa.  Franklin, Butler, and Grundy Counties were hard hit.  Numerous roads and highways were under water and closed as 4 to over 7 inches of rain fell in under six hours.  The heaviest rainfall total was in the Aplington Area in Butler County.  A total of 7.5 inches fell there.  Severe flooding took place in the New Hartford area.  Near record high river stages prompted the evacuation of the town.  In Parkersburg, 1500 to 2000 feet of railroad tracks were washed out.  There was some hail with the storms.  Most of them had small hail, though no significant hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage to a house in Story City.  The sunroom blew off and over the house, then landed in a yard across the street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front extended from a surface low pressure in northwest Kansas to just north of Omaha and then into central Illinois on May 29, 2013. Plentiful moisture was noted south of this front with dewpoints in the 60s.  A strengthening low level jet to 50 knots impinging on the front developed numerous thunderstorms across the area. These storms produced damaging wind, large hail and flash flooding.  The emergency manager in Linn county reported the town of Walker sustained major damage from severe flash flooding, mostly due to Blue Creek in town. Between 50 and 100 homes were damaged, a couple of businesses were destroyed and bridges were under water. About 5 inches of rain fell in about 2 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported several roads closed with running water between Edgewood and Greeley to Manchester.  Also street flooding and water in low lying areas was reported.

The greatest damage was from 3 miles south of Newhall, where a machine shed and garage were completely destroyed, to about 3 miles northwest of Atkins, where significant damage occurred to outbuildings.  The emergency manager in Buchanan county reported a downburst with winds more than 70 mph struck Independence, Iowa. The northwest side of town sustained the most damage with trees down, power poles snapped and signs blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple 1 foot diameter trees were snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure was located in western South Dakota with a stationary front extending into eastern Missouri on May 26-27, 2013.  North of the front temperatures were only in the 50s in eastern Iowa but to the south readings were in the 80s with dewpoints in the 60s. A strengthening low level jet impinging on the frontal boundary helped to trigger numerous training thunderstorms across the area. This led to flash flooding in many areas with roads closed.  In Louisa county a trained spotter reported Highway 92 between Cotter and Columbus Junction had water flowing across it in many locations. One section had large debris washed onto the highway that included railroad ties and logs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported Duck Creek out of it's banks and flowing across Eastern Avenue in Davenport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into Iowa during the afternoon from the northwest.  The airmass became very unstable ahead of the front.  Temperatures rose into the upper 80s to low 90s, with dewpoint readings in the upper 60s to lower 70s.  CAPE increased to 4000 J/kg by late afternoon with a lifted index between -10 and -18 C.  Downdraft CAPE was between 1200 and 1700 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere in the 700 to 900 J/kg range.  The freezing level was very high, around 15,000 feet, with the LCL at 1250 meters.  Deep moisture was available for the system, with precipitable water values around 1.6 inches.  Little shear was available with only 20 kts of effective shear.  A line of thunderstorms formed along the cold front during the afternoon and tracked southeast into Iowa.  Several of the storms produced hail from the size of quarters to the size of golf balls.  Funnel clouds were also reported with a few of the storms.  As the thunderstorms continued into the later afternoon and evening, wind became a more dominant factor.  Numerous trees were downed by high winds in Wright and Franklin Counties.  The storms developed southwest with a line of significant thunderstorms extending from northeast into central Iowa by early to mid evening as new storms formed on the outflow boundary of the original development.  The storms continued to produce quarter to golf ball size hail and wind gusts of 60 to 70 MPH.  The winds caused spotty building damage, including a barn near Story City.  Six horses that were inside were killed.  The barn was east of Boone, where buildings and power lines were destroyed at the Central Iowa Expo grounds.  Two inch diameter hail was reported in parts of Wright and Boone Counties.  A heat burst produced 73 MPH winds in Marion County at the Knoxville Airport.  The temperature rose from 70 to 84 in 15 minutes, and the dewpoint dropped from 63 to 48 in 10 minutes.

A low pressure area tracked east out of South Dakota during the afternoon and evening.  A warm front extending from the low across west central into southeast Iowa lifted north with thunderstorm development during the afternoon.  Thunderstorms formed during the late afternoon and overnight hours as the cold front trailing form the low moved into Iowa  The atmosphere became moderately unstable during the afternoon and evening as moisture continued to pool near the warm front and ahead of the low.  Precipitable water values increased to 1.8 inches by evening.  The CAPE increased to 2000 to 3000 J/kg with 600 to 900 J/kg available in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere, though the freezing level was quite high at 15,000 feet.  Downdraft CAPE was in the 800 to 1000 J/kg range.  The atmosphere had 40 to 50 kts of shear available. Thunderstorms moved into western Iowa and became severe.  Initially the storms produced hail.  Most of the hail was in the 1 to 1.5 inch range, with a few locations approaching 2 inch diameter hail.  Upscale development took place with the storms developing into an MCC before midnight.  The storms then became producers of high high winds and heavy rainfall.  A 67 MPH wind gust was recorded in the Carroll area.  One storm west of Pocahontas produced a 2 mile long narrow path of damage as microburst winds estimated around 90 MPH damaged several houses.  Roofing was lost with debris embedded in exterior walls.  Farm equipment was also blown over.  Flash flooding took place in west central Iowa and parts of central Iowa as reports of 3 to 7 inches of rain in some locations produced flash flooding.  Many highways were closed by high water, and countless county roads were flooded in Carroll, Guthrie, Greene, and Madison Counties.  Subsequent flooding in Madison County caused considerable damage with several bridges washed out or underwater northwest of Bevington.

A nearly stalled frontal boundary across the area interacted with a rather strong upper air disturbance on May 28, 2013, which triggered severe thunderstorms in the moderate to high instability.  Sufficient deep layer shear was in place along with a moist airmass.  This led to reports of damaging wind, large hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and branches were reported down all across the city of Fairfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front extended from a surface low pressure in northwest Kansas to just north of Omaha and then into central Illinois on May 29, 2013. Plentiful moisture was noted south of this front with dewpoints in the 60s.  A strengthening low level jet to 50 knots impinging on the front developed numerous thunderstorms across the area. These storms produced damaging wind, large hail and flash flooding.  The emergency manager in Linn county reported the town of Walker sustained major damage from severe flash flooding, mostly due to Blue Creek in town. Between 50 and 100 homes were damaged, a couple of businesses were destroyed and bridges were under water. About 5 inches of rain fell in about 2 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported the town of Walker sustained major damage from severe flash flooding, mostly due to Blue Creek in town. Between 50 and 100 homes were damaged, a couple of businesses were destroyed and bridges were under water. About 5 inches of rain fell in about 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable airmass on May 19, 2013 led to the development of severe thunderstorms that produced large hail and damaging winds. Temperatures were in the 80s with dewpoints around 70 with a mid level speed maximum rotating around the base of a developing upper low in the Plains states. This increased the deep layer shear during the afternoon and evening hours.  Some of the storms produced golf to baseball size hail and 70 to 80 mph winds.  A trained spotter reported baseball size hail knocked out sky lights at the Walmart store in northeast Keokuk. Golfball to baseball size hail was reported on the north end of the city, with nickel to quarter size hail on the south end of the city.  A downburst produced wind damage in Benton county from the county line 3.5 miles southeast of Blairstown to 2.8 miles southeast of Shellsburg. There was a mile wide swath of near continuous damage to farm outbuildings, tree branches and some power poles. The greatest damage was from 3 miles south of Newhall, where a machine shed and garage were completely destroyed, to about 3 miles northwest of Atkins, where significant damage occurred to outbuildings.  The emergency manager in Buchanan county reported a downburst with winds more than 70 mph struck Independence, Iowa. The northwest side of town sustained the most damage with trees down, power poles snapped and signs blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported widespread wind damage across Linn county which occurred between 1040 pm and 1115 pm.  There was tree damage in Cedar Rapids and three large road signs were blown down on Interstate 380 near Wright Brothers exit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushing into a very moist and unstable airmass triggered numerous severe thunderstorms on May 30, 2013.  Deep layer shear was moderate to strong and freezing levels were in the 11-12k foot level, which was ideal for large hail. The storms produced damaging winds, large hail and several weak tornadoes.  An EF1 tornado touched down in Andalusia, IL around 445 pm CDT and traveled due north. This weak tornado crossed the Mississippi River and moved through eastern sections of Buffalo, IA. Wind speeds were estimated to be 95 mph. Some large trees were snapped and uprooted along the path. Some of the fallen trees fell onto houses.  In extreme northeast Missouri, wind gusts estimated to be 70 mph produced heavy tree damage throughout Scotland County. Several outbuildings were damaged and about 30 power poles were snapped. The tree damage was mainly located between Bible Grove and Memphis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported no power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused flooding of the Little Sioux and Ocheyedan Rivers in northwest Iowa from May 26th to May 30th. The flooding was major to record along parts of the Little Sioux River with the most significant impact in the Cherokee area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor to moderate flooding of the Little Sioux River, with the greatest flooding effect in the southern part of the county. Farmland and a few roads were affected. The river crested at 2.10 feet above flood stage at Spencer on May 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small tornado developed east of Garnavillo (Clayton County) and traveled east/northeast to the Mississippi River before crossing over into Wisconsin. This tornado was part of a larger severe weather outbreak that occurred across much of northeast Iowa during the afternoon of May 29th. These storms developed in a warm and moist airmass behind a warm front that was moving north across the Upper Midwest. In addition to the tornado, a farm east of Oelwein (Fayette County) was hit hard by straight line winds and flash flooding occurred in Protivin (Howard County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pole barn was damaged and numerous trees were knocked down from east of Garnavillo to the Wisconsin state line in the Mississippi River east of Clayton.

The outflow boundary from the previous night's convection was draped across southern Iowa east to west.  As the low level jet increased to 30 kts during the evening, thunderstorms broke out north of the boundary and moved across central and southern Iowa.  The atmosphere was unstable with 2000 to 3000 J/kg of elevated CAPE.  There was 1000 J/kg of downdraft CAPE and 600 J/lg in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere.  The atmosphere was weakly sheared with about 20 kts of shear available.  Precipitable water increased to 1.7 inches by evening.  Thunderstorms that developed north of the boundary produced heavy rainfall and marginal hail due the the weak shear parameters.  The thunderstorms continued through the night.  Severe weather was limited.  Thunderstorm winds broke trees and branches in Story County.  Heavy rainfall occurred in parts of central into west central Iowa.  Rainfall of 5.5 inches fell in a six hour period in Guthrie County.  Flash flooding occurred in Audubon County where a bridge was overtopped by water and several telephone poles stacked beside the road were washed onto the road. Heavy rainfall over the area also caused the Middle River to overflow its banks and flood portions of Maple Ave in southern Guthrie County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inch diameter tree broken and two six inch diameter branched down.  Many smaller branches in the yard.

A downburst produced wind damage in Benton county from the county line 3.5 miles southeast of Blairstown to 2.8 miles southeast of Shellsburg. There was a mile wide swath of near continuous damage to farm outbuildings, tree branches and some power poles. The greatest damage was from 3 miles south of Newhall, where a machine shed and garage were completely destroyed, to about 3 miles northwest of Atkins, where significant damage occurred to outbuildings.  The emergency manager in Buchanan county reported a downburst with winds more than 70 mph struck Independence, Iowa. The northwest side of town sustained the most damage with trees down, power poles snapped and signs blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported power lines down between Delaware and Delhi and a tree was down across the highway just west of Manchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable airmass on May 19, 2013 led to the development of severe thunderstorms that produced large hail and damaging winds. Temperatures were in the 80s with dewpoints around 70 with a mid level speed maximum rotating around the base of a developing upper low in the Plains states. This increased the deep layer shear during the afternoon and evening hours.  Some of the storms produced golf to baseball size hail and 70 to 80 mph winds. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sheriff's Department reported a camper blown over on top of a truck on 300th Street.

A large convective complex developed to the northwest of Iowa during the night of the 12th into the morning of the 13th.  Precipitable water values were very high, in excess of 2.1 inches over Minnesota.  Thunderstorms began to turn south into Iowa in the morning and continue to feed into the inflow of moisture.  In spite of the precipitable water values being much lower over Iowa, moisture convergence was strong making the storms very efficient rain producers.  Three to six inches of rain fall over parts of north central IA.  Some of the heavier rainfall, 4.75 to 5.5 inches, fell in the Lake Mills area of Winnebago County.  The heavy rain caused flash flooding in Worth County with several streets flooded.  Basement flooding was extensive in the town of Fertile.  Flash flooding was also reported in parts of Winnebago County.  Due to the relatively dry soil condition at the beginning of the rainfall, flooding was not as serious as would normally be expected.  General flooding continued into the night in Winnebago County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The rainfall since 0800 was 4.3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a warm frontal boundary, reinforced by an old outflow boundary, was located over northern Iowa.  The atmosphere was relatively unstable with elevated CAPE around 1500 J/kg.  CAPE available in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was in the 600 to 800 J/kg range.  The atmosphere was weakly sheared with about 30 kts of shear available.  A broad low level jet of 40 kts transported moisture north across the boundary with 1.6 inches of precipitable water present.  A line of thunderstorms formed across northwest into north central Iowa.  The storms produced heavy rain and hail up to golf ball in size.  After midnight, thunderstorms moved in from Nebraska.  These storms formed ahead of an approaching cold front.  A 58 MPH wind gust was recorded as the storms moved through the Estherville area.  The storms continued across the state and produced 40 to 50 MPH wind gusts and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report was relayed by KIMT TV in Mason City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area tracked east out of South Dakota during the afternoon and evening.  A warm front extending from the low across west central into southeast Iowa lifted north with thunderstorm development during the afternoon.  Thunderstorms formed during the late afternoon and overnight hours as the cold front trailing form the low moved into Iowa  The atmosphere became moderately unstable during the afternoon and evening as moisture continued to pool near the warm front and ahead of the low.  Precipitable water values increased to 1.8 inches by evening.  The CAPE increased to 2000 to 3000 J/kg with 600 to 900 J/kg available in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere, though the freezing level was quite high at 15,000 feet.  Downdraft CAPE was in the 800 to 1000 J/kg range.  The atmosphere had 40 to 50 kts of shear available. Thunderstorms moved into western Iowa and became severe.  Initially the storms produced hail.  Most of the hail was in the 1 to 1.5 inch range, with a few locations approaching 2 inch diameter hail.  Upscale development took place with the storms developing into an MCC before midnight.  The storms then became producers of high high winds and heavy rainfall.  A 67 MPH wind gust was recorded in the Carroll area.  One storm west of Pocahontas produced a 2 mile long narrow path of damage as microburst winds estimated around 90 MPH damaged several houses.  Roofing was lost with debris embedded in exterior walls.  Farm equipment was also blown over.  Flash flooding took place in west central Iowa and parts of central Iowa as reports of 3 to 7 inches of rain in some locations produced flash flooding.  Many highways were closed by high water, and countless county roads were flooded in Carroll, Guthrie, Greene, and Madison Counties.  Subsequent flooding in Madison County caused considerable damage with several bridges washed out or underwater northwest of Bevington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into Iowa during the afternoon from the northwest.  The airmass became very unstable ahead of the front.  Temperatures rose into the upper 80s to low 90s, with dewpoint readings in the upper 60s to lower 70s.  CAPE increased to 4000 J/kg by late afternoon with a lifted index between -10 and -18 C.  Downdraft CAPE was between 1200 and 1700 J/kg, with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere in the 700 to 900 J/kg range.  The freezing level was very high, around 15,000 feet, with the LCL at 1250 meters.  Deep moisture was available for the system, with precipitable water values around 1.6 inches.  Little shear was available with only 20 kts of effective shear.  A line of thunderstorms formed along the cold front during the afternoon and tracked southeast into Iowa.  Several of the storms produced hail from the size of quarters to the size of golf balls.  Funnel clouds were also reported with a few of the storms.  As the thunderstorms continued into the later afternoon and evening, wind became a more dominant factor.  Numerous trees were downed by high winds in Wright and Franklin Counties.  The storms developed southwest with a line of significant thunderstorms extending from northeast into central Iowa by early to mid evening as new storms formed on the outflow boundary of the original development.  The storms continued to produce quarter to golf ball size hail and wind gusts of 60 to 70 MPH.  The winds caused spotty building damage, including a barn near Story City.  Six horses that were inside were killed.  The barn was east of Boone, where buildings and power lines were destroyed at the Central Iowa Expo grounds.  Two inch diameter hail was reported in parts of Wright and Boone Counties.  A heat burst produced 73 MPH winds in Marion County at the Knoxville Airport.  The temperature rose from 70 to 84 in 15 minutes, and the dewpoint dropped from 63 to 48 in 10 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many 12 inch diameter tree limbs downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into northwest Iowa during the mid to late afternoon hours.  The airmass became unstable by the early afternoon with CAPE in the 2000 to 3000 J/kg range.  Downdraft CAPE was between 800 and 1200 J/kg.  The freezing level was around 13,000 feet.  Precipitable water was around 1.3 to 1.4 inches.  Shear was moderate with 30 to 40 kts of effective shear.  The LCL was quite high around 1750 meters due to dry air in the low levels of the atmosphere.  Thunderstorms developed along the cold front over north central Iowa initially.  The storms produced very heavy rainfall with several reports of penny to nickel size hail received.  One storm produced quarter to half dollar size hail in Hancock County.  Storms became more numerous by early evening.  Many produced small hail.  One storm in Pocahontas County produced hail up to quarter size.  Very heavy rainfall occurred over parts of north central Iowa.  The storms produced around 2 inches of rain in an hour.  More than 3 inches of rain fell in the Aplington area of Butler County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushing into a very moist and unstable airmass triggered numerous severe thunderstorms on May 30, 2013.  Deep layer shear was moderate to strong and freezing levels were in the 11-12k foot level, which was ideal for large hail. The storms produced damaging winds, large hail and several weak tornadoes.  An EF1 tornado touched down in Andalusia, IL around 445 pm CDT and traveled due north. This weak tornado crossed the Mississippi River and moved through eastern sections of Buffalo, IA. Wind speeds were estimated to be 95 mph. Some large trees were snapped and uprooted along the path. Some of the fallen trees fell onto houses.  In extreme northeast Missouri, wind gusts estimated to be 70 mph produced heavy tree damage throughout Scotland County. Several outbuildings were damaged and about 30 power poles were snapped. The tree damage was mainly located between Bible Grove and Memphis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS employee reported a 6 inch diameter tree limb down near Middle Road and the Chateau Knoll Apartments in Bettendorf.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding of the Ocheyedan and Little Sioux Rivers from June 6th to June 18th. Lowlands along the river were affected, including some farmland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor farmland and other lowland flooding along the Little Sioux River. The river crested at 1.7 feet above flood stage at Spencer on June 10th.

Flash flooding took place in west central Iowa and parts of central Iowa as reports of 3 to 7 inches of rain in some locations produced flash flooding.  Many highways were closed by high water, and countless county roads were flooded in Carroll, Guthrie, Greene, and Madison Counties.  Subsequent flooding in Madison County caused considerable damage with several bridges washed out or underwater northwest of Bevington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over A Avenue along the Guthrie/Carroll County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large convective complex developed to the northwest of Iowa during the night of the 12th into the morning of the 13th.  Precipitable water values were very high, in excess of 2.1 inches over Minnesota.  Thunderstorms began to turn south into Iowa in the morning and continue to feed into the inflow of moisture.  In spite of the precipitable water values being much lower over Iowa, moisture convergence was strong making the storms very efficient rain producers.  Three to six inches of rain fall over parts of north central IA.  Some of the heavier rainfall, 4.75 to 5.5 inches, fell in the Lake Mills area of Winnebago County.  The heavy rain caused flash flooding in Worth County with several streets flooded.  Basement flooding was extensive in the town of Fertile.  Flash flooding was also reported in parts of Winnebago County.  Due to the relatively dry soil condition at the beginning of the rainfall, flooding was not as serious as would normally be expected.  General flooding continued into the night in Winnebago County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 4.7 inches through the morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm airmass was in place over Iowa.  A cold front moved southeast into the state during the afternoon and early evening hours.  Temperatures ahead of the front were in the 90s, with dewpoints in the upper 60s to low 70s.  There was little shear with 20 kts available.  CAPE was around 2500 J/kg, with downdraft CAPE of 1200 J/kg.  The freezing level was quite high, just under 15,000 feet with CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere around 600 J/kg.  Isolated thunderstorms formed ahead of the cold front.  One storm northeast of Albia in Monroe County pulsed and became severe.  One inch diameter hail was reported with this cell.

A low pressure area tracked east out of South Dakota during the afternoon and evening.  A warm front extending from the low across west central into southeast Iowa lifted north with thunderstorm development during the afternoon.  Thunderstorms formed during the late afternoon and overnight hours as the cold front trailing form the low moved into Iowa  The atmosphere became moderately unstable during the afternoon and evening as moisture continued to pool near the warm front and ahead of the low.  Precipitable water values increased to 1.8 inches by evening.  The CAPE increased to 2000 to 3000 J/kg with 600 to 900 J/kg available in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere, though the freezing level was quite high at 15,000 feet.  Downdraft CAPE was in the 800 to 1000 J/kg range.  The atmosphere had 40 to 50 kts of shear available. Thunderstorms moved into western Iowa and became severe.  Initially the storms produced hail.  Most of the hail was in the 1 to 1.5 inch range, with a few locations approaching 2 inch diameter hail.  Upscale development took place with the storms developing into an MCC before midnight.  The storms then became producers of high high winds and heavy rainfall.  A 67 MPH wind gust was recorded in the Carroll area.  One storm west of Pocahontas produced a 2 mile long narrow path of damage as microburst winds estimated around 90 MPH damaged several houses.  Roofing was lost with debris embedded in exterior walls.  Farm equipment was also blown over.  Flash flooding took place in west central Iowa and parts of central Iowa as reports of 3 to 7 inches of rain in some locations produced flash flooding.  Many highways were closed by high water, and countless county roads were flooded in Carroll, Guthrie, Greene, and Madison Counties.  Subsequent flooding in Madison County caused considerable damage with several bridges washed out or underwater northwest of Bevington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusts estimated at 60 to 65 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable airmass on May 19, 2013 led to the development of severe thunderstorms that produced large hail and damaging winds. Temperatures were in the 80s with dewpoints around 70 with a mid level speed maximum rotating around the base of a developing upper low in the Plains states. This increased the deep layer shear during the afternoon and evening hours.  Some of the storms produced golf to baseball size hail and 70 to 80 mph winds.  A trained spotter reported baseball size hail knocked out sky lights at the Walmart store in northeast Keokuk. Golfball to baseball size hail was reported on the north end of the city, with nickel to quarter size hail on the south end of the city.  A downburst produced wind damage in Benton county from the county line 3.5 miles southeast of Blairstown to 2.8 miles southeast of Shellsburg. There was a mile wide swath of near continuous damage to farm outbuildings, tree branches and some power poles. The greatest damage was from 3 miles south of Newhall, where a machine shed and garage were completely destroyed, to about 3 miles northwest of Atkins, where significant damage occurred to outbuildings.  The emergency manager in Buchanan county reported a downburst with winds more than 70 mph struck Independence, Iowa. The northwest side of town sustained the most damage with trees down, power poles snapped and signs blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report relayed by the Scott county sheriff's office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front extended from a surface low pressure in northwest Kansas to just north of Omaha and then into central Illinois on May 29, 2013. Plentiful moisture was noted south of this front with dewpoints in the 60s.  A strengthening low level jet to 50 knots impinging on the front developed numerous thunderstorms across the area. These storms produced damaging wind, large hail and flash flooding.  The emergency manager in Linn county reported the town of Walker sustained major damage from severe flash flooding, mostly due to Blue Creek in town. Between 50 and 100 homes were damaged, a couple of businesses were destroyed and bridges were under water. About 5 inches of rain fell in about 2 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported 10 inch tree limbs down in Edgewood along with power out in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak warm air advection pattern was in place across Iowa. Precipitable water values had increased to 1.6 inches over southern Iowa by early afternoon.  An area of thunderstorms formed in a weakly sheared environment.  The CAPE was around 1000 J/kg, with shear around 25 kts.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was 400 J/kg.  The storms produced very heavy rain and some hail as they moved through.  A rainfall total of 3.57 inches was reported west of Chariton, in Lucas County, much of which fell in an hour.  Nearly all streets in the town of Chariton were flooded in places.  The outflow boundary from the convection over southern Iowa moved west and set off additional thunderstorms over western Iowa.  The severe environment was move favorable in western Iowa.  CAPE had increased to between 2000 and 3000 J/kg, with downdraft CAPE around 1200 J/kg.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was around 400 J/kg with the freezing level around 14,000 feet.  Shear remained weak at around 25 kts.  Isolated thunderstorms developed over west central and southwest Iowa.  One of the stronger storms produced golf ball size hail east of Audubon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 3.57 inches, most of which was in the past hour.
